item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with this. It's excessive. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm usually a sucker for exploration-SCPs, and I really like a couple of the things you do here: the shorthand naming convention strikes me as something that's reasonably easy to follow1, and I adore the use of a simultaneous perspectives in the form of a multi-squad approach2.

Unfortunately, I would have to agree with previous poster's assessments in that aside from those minor traits, I can't find any redeemable qualities in this submission. This comes across as incredibly shallow in terms of story-depth, sorely lacking in characterization of both the anomaly and actual characters, and poorly paced regarding the flow of the narrative.

All this, quite obviously, comes to a head in the logs. It's so over-concerned with presenting the hectic deterioration of order at the hands of the anomaly, that very, very little happens that has any actual substance. I'm all for a good meat-grinder, but when all I'm presented with is gunfire and gore ad nauseum, I can't help but groan and eyeroll. It's like watching a fight scene excised from a film without a supporting narrative. Sure, it might be cool to watch divorced from any context if the SFX or choreography is particularly inventive or bombastic, but if it's not, it's just not entertaining.

That's the case here. The action and deaths aren't particularly imaginative or novel, and as that comprises pretty much the entire 'story', I'm left pining for the missing elements that would better support the (rote) action elements.

This would greatly benefit by pumping the brakes a few times throughout, I feel. More time getting to know just a touch about the characters, more time to give a stronger sense of space/environment/asthetic to establish the setting, more time on the investigative aspects that allow one to further build the story they're trying to convey.

That's what it all really boils down to — time. Things quickly escalate up to 11, and then never really come back down. It very quickly rises, but there really isn't much falling action, creating an imbalance in structure that ultimately makes it less of a story and more of a series of events. It's unrewarding.

Footnotes
1. where a glut of actual names mixed with callsigns can sometimes obfuscate who's speaking — there's been one or two times where I'd have to scroll back up in other works to be reminded of who each member was/their specialized role
2. something I've attempted myself in a development-hell WIP. I don't think it's pulled off quite as well as it could be here, but it's a stylistic choice I appreciate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’m sorry, but I don’t see anything interesting here. It’s a bog standard tale of an MTF unit being wiped out by a generic anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, I apologize. I don't know how I missed that. Incendiary rounds make sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So to address your first point I'd like to point out the time at which this takes place, in 1947. I imagine the foundation not being as capable as super organization we know and love.

Its also a slight nitpick of mine when the MTF has nothing but convential gunfire. At least something incendiary! I feel so many anomalies would be resistant to typical weapons they would carry something better

That's literally what they do. They use incendiary rounds and grenades.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoy the idea behind this one. The idea of a many faced tree shouting angrily at me in a foreign language is genuinely unnerving and I like the grainy image of the tree creatures.

That being said, my interest does begin to wane whenever I see the usual "MTF shows up to fight anomaly, is woefully underprepared, and dies miserably" log as others stated. Ive always felt the Foundation would be more capable as a whole, especially after losing what would apparently be countless MTF members.

Its also a slight nitpick of mine when the MTF has nothing but convential gunfire. At least something incendiary! I feel so many anomalies would be resistant to typical weapons they would carry something better.

Of course I dont every MTF log to just consist of them kicking everything out of the water, I just feel theyre overwhelmed a bit too much.

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
Okay, let's do this.

+ Nitpicks
– hide block

Artistic depiction of John Chapman, made in 1860

Not really a fan of the word 'made'. Maybe use 'created' or something more specific to art?

Due to the circumstance of SCP-4608's recovery, no other containment procedures were developed.

I'm not entirely sure that this is needed. It doesn't hurt the article with its existence, but surely this is just implied already by the Object Class and the word 'Archived' in conprocs. However, this is definitely a personal note.

On October 14, 1947, the Foundation was alerted to the occurrences surrounding SCP-4608 by police reports of strange creatures being seen in the vicinity of SCP-4608

I don't like this sentence; it seems repetitive in the way it's written. Each section is super similar and it feels clunky.

What the fuck would do-

This is a super personal one, but I much prefer using — to show interruption. The additional length works better for me.

the tree line

A tree line is generally a specific term for a height above sea level, after which trees cannot survive. They're often found on mountains. I suggest that you use a different wording, even though this technically isn't incorrect.

What do you mean— OH GOD.

I don't like the capitalisation here, especially as you've used exclamation points to do the same thing in other areas.

What the fuck! Those things came out of nowhere. What's the plan, Regis.

Use interrobangs. I'm not completely sure about the 'what the fuck' as it can possibly be said in a non-questioning way, but the 'What's the plan, Regis' should definitely have a question mark at the end.

Following debriefing and receiving a psychological evaluation,

"Following… recieving a psychological evaluation" is not grammatically correct. Just removing 'receiving' would be good enough.

– hide block
+ Overall
– hide block

I really enjoyed this. The dialogue felt realistic all the way through, both with what was said and how it was said. At no point did I feel it was robotic.

I was hooked all the way through this. It's the perfect length, and the narrative and anomaly are both super interesting. Everything about this seems like it was a conscious decision. The image of the -1 instance is great, as it's blurred in a way that makes it seem realistic. The only thing that I can think of for constructive criticism is that I felt like -2 could've possibly been more involved. Granted, it's definitely there at the end, but that section of the log feels like it may be a tiny bit short. This didn't really deduct from my enjoyment of this in any way, though.

All in all, the gorax is great, and I feel super confident upvoting. Keep up the great work!

– hide block

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to gee0765 or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Crab apples of the crabbiest degree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well to each their own, I'm thankful you gave it a read regardless and will keep this in mind if I write something similar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’d agree, honestly. I find the logs to drag a bit as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't decide whether they'd respond to Dr. King poorly or positively, but he definitely needs some sort of callback to this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably to avoid the PTSD. Experience might not be as useful if he has a panic attack every time he sees a tree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i fucking murder people
for the trees
of course.

item.title: Re: Cool person right here ^
item.content:encoded: I thought that might be intentional. actually i might consider ramping it up even more — more goofy catchphrases, more dunking on each other during the banter sections, that sort of thing. specificity is the key either way!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The exploration/combat/etc. log feels fairly pointless to me. The first section doesn't really establish anything we don't already know and is filled with the same early-mission banter that is in every one of these things. The second also doesn't really establish anything new and also has fairly standard slow diminishing of the character list. I didn't feel attached enough to the characters for their fates to feel meaningful. The last has a few pieces of new stuff, but it didn't feel proportional compared to how much came before it.

I'm admittedly starting from a place of not particularly enjoying this format of conveying action, but that section left me with a particularly strong feeling of having read it before. I'm not sure if what's being portrayed there adds enough to justify that.

item.title: Cool person right here ^
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! And thanks for the typo fixes!

Cool on you for picking up the alien reference.

And the grunt nature is well intended, the MSF is supposed to feel like a Foundation commando type unit, but i may make some adjustments to tone it back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I haven't read this in depth yet, will come back and edit this comment when I have. But I am disappointed: no reference to our old friend Dr. King? Not even once? (Lol, I'm kidding, can't wait to read this. Reaction to come shortly.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
well thanks now I'm going to scream and flee the next time someone offers me an apple. +1

More specifically: Creepy as hell, man. The dialogue can be a little on-the-nose Aliens (e.g. "I got a bad feeling about this one") but the scenario is so fucking surreal and horrible that it works nonetheless. If you give this another scan, I might work on making at least a couple of them feel more "specific"; I like the Fives/Sixes/etc. codename usage but it does also tend to lead to them feeling same-y, which isn't helped by the aforementioned Aliens kick-ass-grunt affectation.

On a more technical note, I made a variety of edits throughout the transcripts for grammar, consistency, and fixing some typos. Might be worth another once-over to clean up some more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's one fucked up lorax +1

item.title: Site Criticism Team - Junior Staff Post - Info
item.content:encoded:
NomR2, unlike in the forums, collapsing of long mainsite posts is not required. Use of collapsibles in an SCP is down to personal choices by the author.

In addition, on a more personal note, it's not an issue that I have to scroll down on a posted work. In the forums, I may not need to or want to read through a specific super long forum post/reply. However, on the mainsite, I am clicking on a post in order to read it. Therefore, I don't see why an easy method to skip the entire point of the SCP is necessary.
Oh, what I wouldn't give to read this with fresh eyes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can You Fold This, Its To Long For Mobile Users

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only thing that bugs me here is that the survivor is getting mind-wiped, all that experience just gone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A+ execution on the log there. It only said what it needed to and the personnel react in a way that feels natural.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The usual high aki quality. Good job mate.
